coredata.dc:title: Camellia oleifera derived carbon dots modified TiO2 as sensor for the rapid detection of 4-nitrophenol in wastewater
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhan, Peiying
Xu, Xueli
coredata.dc:description: This paper presents an innovative electrochemical sensor utilizing carbon dots modified with titanium dioxide (TiO2-CDs) for the rapid and accurate detection of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) in wastewater. This sensor offers a cost-effective and efficient approach to on-site monitoring of 4-NP, a highly toxic and persistent environmental pollutant. The modification of the electrode material with TiO2-CDs significantly enhances the sensor's performance by improving sensitivity, selectivity, and stability. The synthesis of TiO2-CDs involves the integration of carbon dots derived from biomass with TiO2, resulting in a composite material with an enlarged surface area and enhanced photocatalytic properties. The unique hollow spherical structure of the TiO2-CDs further amplifies their photon absorption capacity. Electrochemical characterization validates the successful modification of the electrode surface and demonstrates the electrocatalytic activity of the TiO2-CDs modified electrode towards 4-NP. The sensor exhibits a linear correlation between the peak oxidation current and the concentration of 4-NP, enabling precise and reliable quantification. Notably, the sensor's detection range spans from 10 μM to 350 μM, with an impressively low detection limit of 0.12 μM. Real sample analysis of wastewater underscores the sensor's practical utility, revealing high recovery rates and minimal relative standard deviations. The development of this electrochemical sensor, based on TiO2-CDs, presents a promising solution for the swift and accurate detection of 4-NP in wastewater, offering potential advancements in pollution control, environmental monitoring, and wastewater treatment strategies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical sensor
Carbon dots
TiO2 modification
4-nitrophenol
Wastewater

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of hydrothermal carbon spheres with phenolization

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (A) carbon sphere and (B) TiO2-CDs.
(A) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm and (B) BJH pore size distribution curve of the carbon sphere, TiO2 and TiO2-CDs.
XRD patterns of TiO2 and TiO2-CDs.
(A) UV–vis spectra and (B) FTIR spectra of TiO2 and TiO2-CDs.
(A) Nyquist plots and (B) Bode plots of bare GCE, TiO2/GCE, carbon sphere/GCE, TiO2-CDs/GCE in the solution of 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-.

(A) CVs of bare GCE, TiO2/GCE, carbon sphere/GCE and TiO2-CDs/GCE in the PBS solution (pH=6) containing 20 μM 4-NP with a scan rate is 50 mV/s. (B) DPV response of bare GCE, TiO2/GCE, carbon sphere/GCE and TiO2-CDs/GCE in the PBS solution(pH=6) containing 300 μM 4-NP.
The possible reduction mechanism of 4-NP at TiO2-CDs.
(A) DPVs of different concentrations 4-NP on TiO2-CDs/GCE (10, 20, 40, 50, 70, 90, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300 and 350 μM); (B) The relationship curves of I with 4-NP concentration.
Electrochemical performance of various electrochemical sensors for 4-NP detection.
Real sample analysis of 4-NP in waste water (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Camellia oleifera derived carbon dots modified TiO2 as sensor for the rapid detection of 4-nitrophenol in wastewater

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Teaching Affairs, Tonghua Normal University, Tonghua 134001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Teaching Affairs, Tonghua Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Teaching Affairs, Tonghua Normal University, Tonghua 134001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biological and Chemical Engineering, Nanyang Institute of Technology, Nanyang 473004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biological and Chemical Engineering, Nanyang Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biological and Chemical Engineering, Nanyang Institute of Technology, Nanyang 473004 China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper presents an innovative electrochemical sensor utilizing carbon dots modified with titanium dioxide (TiO2-CDs) for the rapid and accurate detection of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) in wastewater. This sensor offers a cost-effective and efficient approach to on-site monitoring of 4-NP, a highly toxic and persistent environmental pollutant. The modification of the electrode material with TiO2-CDs significantly enhances the sensor's performance by improving sensitivity, selectivity, and stability. The synthesis of TiO2-CDs involves the integration of carbon dots derived from biomass with TiO2, resulting in a composite material with an enlarged surface area and enhanced photocatalytic properties. The unique hollow spherical structure of the TiO2-CDs further amplifies their photon absorption capacity. Electrochemical characterization validates the successful modification of the electrode surface and demonstrates the electrocatalytic activity of the TiO2-CDs modified electrode towards 4-NP. The sensor exhibits a linear correlation between the peak oxidation current and the concentration of 4-NP, enabling precise and reliable quantification. Notably, the sensor's detection range spans from 10 μM to 350 μM, with an impressively low detection limit of 0.12 μM. Real sample analysis of wastewater underscores the sensor's practical utility, revealing high recovery rates and minimal relative standard deviations. The development of this electrochemical sensor, based on TiO2-CDs, presents a promising solution for the swift and accurate detection of 4-NP in wastewater, offering potential advancements in pollution control, environmental monitoring, and wastewater treatment strategies.

ce:section.ce:para: Water, often considered the world's most vital resource, is increasingly subjected to anthropogenic pollution resulting from diverse industrial activities. A rising concentration of organic pollutants, particularly 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), presents a significant risk to both aquatic ecosystems and human health [1,2]. 4-NP is a representative persistent organic pollutant, predominantly originating from industrial effluents such as dyes, pesticides, pharmaceuticals, and petrochemicals. The United States Environmental Protection Agency designates it as a priority pollutant due to its pronounced toxicity, potential for bioaccumulation, and recalcitrant characteristics. Consequently, the swift identification and measurement of 4-NP in wastewater are crucial for effective pollution control and management [3–6].

Among the myriad techniques available, electrochemical sensing stands out as a practical, cost-efficient, and effective method for the real-time detection and quantification of 4-NP [7]. The versatility, compactness, and swift response of electrochemical sensors render them particularly suited for on-site assessments [8,9]. Furthermore, they exhibit sensitivity even to minute concentrations of pollutants, thus delivering precise data for prompt intervention [10]. However, traditional electrochemical sensors face limitations such as signal instability, electrode fouling, and diminished selectivity and sensitivity [11–13].

To mitigate these limitations, the modification of electrode materials has emerged as a promising avenue to enhance the efficacy of electrochemical sensors. Numerous materials, including noble metals, metal oxides, polymers, carbon nanotubes, and graphene, have been investigated for electrode enhancements [14,15]. Notably, TiO2, a metal oxide semiconductor, has garnered significant interest owing to its outstanding chemical stability, non-toxic nature, and efficient electron-transfer kinetics [16]. Yet, its utility is restricted by the swift recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and its comparatively modest specific surface area.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs), a carbon-rich material derived from the pyrolysis of biomass in anoxic conditions, have been identified as a potential contender for environmental monitoring [17–20]. This study aims to introduce an innovative electrochemical sensor utilizing TiO2-CDs for the expedited detection of 4-NP in wastewater. Emphasis will be placed on the synthesis of TiO2-CDs, characterization of their physicochemical attributes, design of the TiO2-modified electrochemical sensor, and examination of its sensitivity, selectivity, stability, and practical applicability in detecting 4-NP.

The potential groundbreaking nature of this research might pave the way for novel developments in environmental monitoring, underscoring the considerable potential of biomass-derived materials in augmenting the efficacy of electrochemical sensors. This could present a cost-effective, efficient, and environmentally-friendly approach to wastewater treatment, marking a significant stride in combating water pollution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of hydrothermal carbon spheres with phenolization
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrothermal carbon spheres and carbon quantum dot solutions (CDs) were synthesized using the hydrothermal technique [21–23]. This work employed the fruit of Camellia oleifera, discarding its core and outer skin to retain only the inner shell. This shell was rinsed with deionized water and ethanol, dried at 60 °C for 48 h, and subsequently ground to a particle size of 200 mesh.

A sample of 2.0 g of the 200 mesh Camellia oleifera shell was taken in a 300 mL beaker, to which 0.5 mg of pyrogallol and 100 mL of deionized water were added. Following a 50-minute ultrasonic treatment at 100 W, the shell powder was uniformly dispersed, and the pyrogallol fully dissolved. This solution was then subjected to magnetic stirring for 8 h at 800 r/min. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE)-lined stainless steel reaction vessel (200 mL, KH-100, Yibel Instrument Co., LTD) and heated at a rate of 2 °C/min in a blast drying oven. The reaction proceeded at 230 °C for 48 h before cooling to ambient temperature. Post-reaction, the solution was filtered through a 0.45 µm aqueous membrane, yielding hydrothermal carbon spheres as the residue. These spheres were cleansed with deionized water and ethanol in an ultrasonic microwave extraction instrument until the filtrate was transparent. Once dried at 60 °C, these carbon spheres, termed CDs, were set aside for subsequent applications.

A 200 mg sample of the synthesized hydrothermal carbon spheres was introduced into a beaker with 200 mL of anhydrous ethanol. Subsequent to 30 min of ultrasonic treatment for consistent dispersion, 0.6 mL of a 25%− 28% ammonia solution was incorporated. After stirring magnetically for 30 min, 1.5 mL of tetra-n-butyl titanate (97%) was methodically added dropwise. The beaker was sealed using cling film and agitated at ambient temperature for 24 h. The precipitate that formed was isolated through centrifugation at 8000 r/min, dried at 60 °C for 6 h, and then annealed at 120 °C for 12 h.

The annealed substance was relocated to a muffle furnace, distributed to a thickness of under 1 cm, and heated at a rate of 2 °C/min up to 500 °C for calcination. This process eradicated the carbon sphere template, yielding pure TiO2. Separately, 150 mg of the produced TiO2 was combined with 50 mL of a carbon quantum dot solution in a beaker. A 10-minute ultrasonic treatment ensured the homogeneous dispersion of the TiO2 hollow spheres within the solution, after which it was stirred at room temperature for 6 h. Post-centrifugation, the resultant precipitate was dried at 60 °C for 12 h and annealed at 120 °C for another 12 h, culminating in the production of TiO2-CDs.

The microstructural characterization of the material was conducted using a NovaNanoSEM450 field-emission scanning electron microscope (SEM). The material was dispersed in anhydrous ethanol and, following ultrasonic dispersion, uniformly adhered to a silicon wafer for drying. X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD) using the D8 ADVANCE instrument provided structural insight into the material. The excitation source deployed Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54056λ = 1.54056 Å), operating at a voltage and current of 40 kV and 400 mA, respectively. Scans in the 2θ range extended from 5° to 90°, and the specimen was pre-dried at 60 °C. The optical absorption attributes of the material were assessed via ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV-Vis) using the Lambda 750 S UV/VIS/NIR instrument. Measurements spanned a wavelength range of 200–800 nm at a 0.1 nm resolution, utilizing a barium sulfate backdrop. The material's chemical structure was elucidated using the FW-500 Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy analyzer (FT-IR). Spectra were acquired over a range of 50–4500 cm−1, employing potassium bromide as a reference. The NOVE 2000e instrument, designed for evaluating surface area and pore size, facilitated the analysis of the material's specific surface area and porosity through nitrogen adsorption-desorption tests. After preconditioning the sample at 60 °C under vacuum for 20 min and subsequently at 120 °C for 6 h, nitrogen gas was used for the adsorption-desorption assessment.

The morphology of the fabricated material was initially examined using SEM. Fig. 1A illustrates that, by using powdered Camellia oleifera fruit shells as the precursor, we successfully synthesized uniformly-shaped hydrothermal carbon spheres with diameters ranging between 1 and 2 µm [24]. Building on this, and employing these hydrothermal carbon spheres as templates, this work adeptly crafted hollow titanium dioxide spheres via the sol-gel process [25,26], as showcased in Fig. 1B. The incorporation of CDs onto the titanium dioxide spheres was realized through mechanical agitation. The observed variations in the sizes of the TiO2-CDs particles can be ascribed to the inherent size disparity of the hydrothermal carbon sphere templates. Notably, the adoption of a spherical configuration markedly augmented the specific surface area of TiO2. This unique structure is expected to contribute significantly to the composite's enhanced surface area and photon absorption capacity, as demonstrated in the nitrogen adsorption-desorption tests and UV-Vis spectra. The formation of CDs can be observed using TEM. As shown in the Fig. 2. A clear CDs can be noticed on the attachment of TiO2.

The structural attributes of the fabricated materials were evaluated using nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and BJH pore size distribution curves, as depicted in Fig. 3A. The determined specific surface areas for the carbon spheres, TiO2, and TiO2-CDs were 18.414 m²/g, 6.986 m²/g, and 32.174 m²/g, respectively. Both anatase TiO2 and TiO2-CDs exhibited type II isotherms without hysteresis loops, suggestive of the non-porous character of these synthesized materials [27,28]. Notably, in contrast to TiO2, the TiO2-CDs manifested a pronounced enhancement in specific surface area, presenting a type IV isotherm accompanied by an H4 hysteresis loop [29]. Such evidence corroborates the effective generation of TiO2 spherical structures utilizing carbon microspheres as templates [30].

Fig. 3B delineates the BJH pore size distribution curves for all three materials. The prominent peak at 3.75 nm is ascribed to void spaces stemming from the clustering of TiO2 nanoparticles. Peaks discerned at 8.21 nm for both TiO2 and TiO2-CDs are indicative of sphere agglomeration, thereby underscoring the successful creation of TiO2 hollow spherical architectures [31]. These insights are consistent with the disparities discerned in the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of the materials [32].

XRD was utilized to ascertain the phase composition of the synthesized materials. The XRD pattern for pure TiO2 displayed distinct diffraction peaks at 25.4°, 38.1°, 48.3°, 54.2°, 55.4°, 62.8°, 70.5°, and 75.3°. These peaks correspond to the (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), (220), and (215) crystal planes, respectively, as illustrated in Fig. 4 [33–35]. These observed peaks are congruent with the anatase-type TiO2 XRD pattern (JCPDS NO.21–1272), confirming that the TiO2 spheres produced using the sol-gel approach are of the anatase phase with commendable crystallinity. The XRD pattern for TiO2-CDs closely mirrors that of pure TiO2, exhibiting minimal, if any, discernible diffraction peaks attributable to the CDs. This observation can be linked to the amorphous character of the carbon quantum dot nanoparticles. Their faint crystal plane diffraction within the 2θ range of 10°− 30° is effectively eclipsed by the proximate defining peaks of TiO2, thereby predominantly revealing the TiO2's characteristic peaks [36]. Furthermore, the sharpness and exclusivity of the diffraction peaks across all materials underscore the high purity and crystalline quality of the materials synthesized.

Fig. 5 A presents the ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance absorption spectra for two materials: TiO2 and TiO2-CDs. Both materials display pronounced light absorption in the ultraviolet region (λ < 400 nm), which originates from electronic transitions from the O2p orbitals to the Ti3d orbitals [37–39]. TiO2 exhibits modest light absorption within the 400–800 nm spectrum. This can be ascribed to the incorporation of nitrogen (N) elements, remnants from ammonia in the synthesis process, and carbon (C) elements sourced from the biomass carbon template [40,41]. During the subsequent calcination at elevated temperatures, C/N doping transpires. Remarkably, the ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance absorption spectrum for the CD-doped material reveals a pronounced redshift. TiO2-CDs showcase robust light absorption between 400 and 700 nm, suggesting that the integration of biomass-derived carbon dots augments the photon absorption efficacy of the material [42]. This enhancement widens its absorption spectrum and promotes the creation of additional photoinduced charge carriers, thereby amplifying the material's photocatalytic performance [43].

The chemical structures of the materials were elucidated using FTIR spectroscopy. As illustrated in Fig. 5B, both TiO2 and TiO2-CDs exhibit broad absorption peaks within the 400–1000 cm−1 range. These peaks can be associated with the stretching vibrations of the Ti-O-Ti covalent bonds present in TiO2 [44]. Peaks discerned at 3350 cm−1 and 1640 cm−1 can be attributed to the bending vibrations of the H-O-H bonds in water molecules. Additionally, the peak manifesting at 2300 cm−1 suggests the potential presence of residual carbon (C) elements, likely remnants from incompletely excised carbon spheres, which would result in the stretching vibrations of OC=O [45].窗体顶端.

Fig. 6 shows the Nyquist plots and Bode plots for various modified electrodes immersed in a 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution. The chromatographic line signifies the impedance spectrum of the GCE, registering a Ret of 93 Ω. The red spectrum corresponds to the TiO2-modified electrode, presenting a Ret of 390 Ω. This elevated Ret can be linked to the semiconductor properties of titanium dioxide, which restrict electron transfer on the electrode interface [46,47]. In contrast, the blue spectrum illustrates the impedance of the carbon sphere-modified electrode, evidencing a diminished impedance relative to the TiO2/GCE. This decrement is likely due to the carbon sphere's enhanced electron transfer velocity and its expansive specific surface area [48]. The green spectrum reveals a Ret of 340 Ω for the TiO2-CDs modified electrode. When juxtaposed with the unadulterated GCE, a moderate rise in impedance is discerned, likely stemming from the diminished conductivity of both TiO2 and carbon spheres relative to GCE [49]. These impedance variations substantiate the successful electrode surface modification using TiO2-CDs. The Bode plots show only two regions which correspond to only two types of elements in the equivalent circuit. The frequency range 1–100 kHz shows a linear plot with an almost negligible change in the impedance against the frequency, depicting the conduction of the ions in the electrolyte. In the frequency range of 1 mHz to 100 Hz, the observed linear plot with a negative slope clearly reveals that the double layer capacitance of the interface with only Warburg impedance dominates the observed signal in this region.

To evaluate the electrocatalytic activity of the modified electrodes for 4-NP, this work tested bare GCE, TiO2/GCE, carbon sphere/GCE, and TiO2-CDs/GCE in a PBS solution containing 20 μM 4-NP at pH 6. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were conducted at a scan rate of 50 mV/s within a potential window of − 0.5–0.8 V. As delineated in Fig. 7 A, the CV profile for 4-NP on the GCE lacks both oxidation and reduction peaks, underscoring the limited electrocatalytic propensity of the unmodified electrode for 4-NP. In contrast, TiO2/GCE reveals a moderate peak, suggesting the inherent catalytic activity of TiO2 particles anchored on the electrode towards 4-NP. Given the negative charges carried by the nitro and hydroxyl groups in 4-NP, they readily adsorb onto TiO2 via electrostatic interactions [50,51], manifesting as discernible oxidation currents. The carbon sphere/GCE also presents moderate peaks, potentially due to mutual aromatic structures, such as benzene rings, in 4-NP and carbon spheres. The π-π stacking between these entities amplifies adsorption, elevating the oxidation-reduction peak intensities [52]. This reveals the inherent electrocatalytic capabilities of both electrodes for 4-NP. Notably, TiO2-CDs/GCE showcases pronounced oxidation and reduction peaks, indicative of its superior catalytic amplification for 4-NP's electrochemical response [53]. This can be ascribed to the synergistic interaction between TiO2 and CDs in the composite, bolstering the electrode-4-NP adsorption [54]. Hence, the oxidation-reduction peak currents for 4-NP on TiO2-CDs/GCE are markedly enhanced. The considerable increase in oxidation and reduction peak currents compared to bare electrodes underscores the advantageous role played by the TiO2-CDs composite in promoting electrochemical reactions. This enhancement can be attributed to the synergistic effects arising from the integration of carbon dots and TiO2, leading to improved adsorption capabilities and electron transfer kinetics.

Subsequent differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) analyses, performed using the aforementioned electrodes in a 300 μM 4-NP solution, affirmed these observations. As displayed in Fig. 7B, the peak current culminates for TiO2-CDs/GCE, corroborating the pronounced catalytic efficacy of the TiO2-CDs composite towards 4-NP [55]. Furthermore, CV assessments in a blank PBS solution yielded negligible oxidation or reduction peaks. However, the presence of 20 μM 4-NP in the PBS solution led to distinct peaks, confirming that the observed electrochemical responses between − 0.5 and 0.8 V are exclusively attributed to 4-NP's electrochemical activity. The considerable increase in specific surface area resulting from the TiO2-CDs composite structure is a significant contributing factor to the improved performance. The TiO2-CDs composite facilitates higher adsorption of 4-NP molecules due to its enlarged surface area and the presence of carbon dots, which might contribute to π-π stacking interactions. The incorporation of TiO2 not only enhances adsorption but also promotes charge separation and transfer during electrochemical processes.

The electro-reduction process of 4-NP on TiO2-CDs was determined to be a two-electron and three-proton mechanism based on our experimental observations ( Fig. 8). In the context of our experimental conditions, the potential reduction mechanism of 4-NP suggests the presence of intermediate products containing free radicals like OH·radical dots, rather than OH− groups. This interpretation is supported by the fact that the presence of OH− ions would lead to the direct generation of H2O as a reduction product under acidic conditions. However, such a scenario would lead to an unbalanced chemical equation. Consequently, it is postulated that the electro-reduction process involves the appearance of free radicals, including OH·radical dots and O·radical dots, as intermediate products.

This work utilized the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) technique to assess the performance of the TiO2-CDs/GCE modified electrode in detecting 4-NP. Fig. 9A presents the DPV response of the TiO2-CDs/GCE modified electrode across different 4-NP concentrations in a pH 6.0 PBS solution. A direct correlation was observed between the increasing concentration and the peak current in the DPV response, while the associated potential values remained largely stable. Fig. 8B delineates a linear correlation between the peak oxidation current and 4-NP concentration, described by the equation i(μA) = 0.6113c(μM) + 3.205 (R2 = 0.996). The method's detection range extends from 10 μM to 350 μM and boasts a detection limit of 0.12 μM. Table 1 contrasts the 4-NP detection performance across various electrochemical sensors. It's worth highlighting that the TiO2-CDs/GCE sensor showcases an expansive detection range coupled with a minimal detection threshold, thus positioning it favorably in terms of detection prowess [56].

To evaluate the practical utility of the TiO2-CDs/GCE, this work tested it with two wastewater samples. These samples were meticulously weighed, any insoluble matter was filtered, and the resultant filtrate was diluted to 100 mL. Three replicate experiments were carried out to determine the 4-NP concentration, with concentrations deduced from a standard curve. The findings are detailed in Table 2. Using the standard addition method, we assessed the recovery rate, which ranged from 97.93% to 103.18%. The RSD was less than 4.02%.

In conclusion, this study presents a novel electrochemical sensor employing TiO2-CDs for the rapid and accurate detection of 4-NP in wastewater. The integration of carbon dots derived from Camellia oleifera biomass with TiO2 resulted in a composite material that exhibited enhanced electrocatalytic activity, sensitivity, and selectivity toward 4-NP. The TiO2-CDs composite demonstrated improved electrocatalytic activity compared to unmodified electrodes. The composite's enlarged surface area, attributed to its unique hollow spherical structure, facilitated higher adsorption of 4-NP molecules, thereby enhancing the sensor's sensitivity. The TiO2-CDs/GCE modified electrode exhibited a linear detection range spanning from 10 μM to 350 μM for 4-NP, surpassing the capabilities of many existing electrochemical sensors. The impressive low detection limit of 0.12 μM signifies the sensor's exceptional sensitivity. Real sample analysis of wastewater samples validated the practical utility of the TiO2-CDs modified electrode for 4-NP detection. The sensor displayed high recovery rates and minimal relative standard deviations, underscoring its accuracy, reliability, and precision in real-world scenarios. The novelty of this work lies in the integration of carbon dots derived from biomass with titanium dioxide, resulting in a unique composite material that combines the advantages of both components. The TiO2-CDs composite exhibited synergistic effects, including enhanced photon absorption capacity, improved electrocatalytic activity, and amplified specific surface area. The developed electrochemical sensor holds great promise for environmental monitoring, pollution control, and wastewater treatment strategies. By providing a swift, accurate, and cost-effective method for 4-NP detection, this sensor contributes to addressing the urgent issue of water pollution and contamination.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bimetallic metal–organic framework derived FeOx/TiO2 embedded in mesoporous carbon nanocomposite for the sensitive electrochemical detection of 4-nitrophenol

Fang, Bimetallic metal–organic framework derived FeOx/TiO2 embedded in mesoporous carbon nanocomposite for the sensitive electrochemical detection of 4-nitrophenol, Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical. 281 (2019) 1063–1072. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.11.083.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design strategy of rGO–HNT–AgNPs based hybrid nanocomposite with enhanced performance for electrochemical detection of 4-nitrophenol

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Developing green sonochemical approaches towards the synthesis of highly integrated and interconnected carbon nanofiber decorated with Sm2O3 nanoparticles and their use in the electrochemical detection of toxic 4-nitrophenol

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical detection and catalytic removal of 4-nitrophenol using CeO2-Cu2O and CeO2-Cu2O/CH nanocomposites

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.B. Khan, K. Akhtar, E.M. Bakhsh, A.M. Asiri, Electrochemical detection and catalytic removal of 4-nitrophenol using CeO2-Cu2O and CeO2-Cu2O/CH nanocomposites, Applied Surface Science. 492 (2019) 726–735. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2019.06.205.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrostatically assembling 2D hierarchical Nb2CTx and zifs-derivatives into Zn-Co-NC nanocage for the electrochemical detection of 4-nitrophenol

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of Co3O4 nanoparticle-decorated porous activated carbon electrode for the electrochemical detection of 4-nitrophenol

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.B. Joseph, J.C. Ezhilarasi, S.-F. Wang, E. Elanthamilan, B. Sriram, J.P. Merlin, Fabrication of Co3O4 nanoparticle-decorated porous activated carbon electrode for the electrochemical detection of 4-nitrophenol, New J. Chem. 45 (2021) 18358–18365. https://doi.org/10.1039/D1NJ02642A.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of silver nanoparticles decorated on core-shell structured tannic acid-coated iron oxide nanospheres for excellent electrochemical detection and efficient catalytic reduction of hazardous 4-nitrophenol

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Sangili, M. Annalakshmi, S.-M. Chen, P. Balasubramanian, M. sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bi-functional Ag-CuxO/g-C3N4 hybrid catalysts for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol and the electrochemical detection of dopamine

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of various deep eutectic solvents on the sustainable synthesis of MgFe2O4 nanoparticles for simultaneous electrochemical determination of nitrofurantoin and 4-Nitrophenol

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.N. Baby, B. Sriram, S.-F. Wang, M. George, Effect of Various Deep Eutectic Solvents on the Sustainable Synthesis of MgFe2O4 Nanoparticles for Simultaneous Electrochemical Determination of Nitrofurantoin and 4-Nitrophenol, ACS Sustainable Chem. sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical detection of 4-Nitrophenol by using graphene based nanocomposite modified glassy carbon electrodes: a mini review
